Section Title: ... > 1. Introduction
Content:
Matching techniques find extensive applications in the field of robotics, facilitating coordination and cooperation among multiple robots ( Khamis, Hussein, & Elmogy, 2015 ). In general, two crucial applications of matching algorithms are task assignment and object manipulation, as discussed in Caccavale and Uchiyama, 2016 , Smith and Bullo, 2009 . In task assignment, algorithms optimize by considering individual robot capabilities and task requirements, ensuring efficient utilization of the robot workforce based on skills, availability, and task complexity. In object manipulation, the collaborative approach of matching enables multiple robots to work synchronously, particularly when paired with complementary skills. This addresses tasks that might be too challenging for a single robot due to complexity or difficulty. A further area where the effective matching plays a significant role is formation control, as described in Sakurama, Azuma, and Sugie (2018) .
 ...

Section Title: ... > 5. Multiple multi-agent systems
Content:
In this section some useful considerations will be provided on how to apply the obtained results to the case of multiple multi-agent systems and on how the properties of the single swarm can ensure further features on all the groups of swarms.

results.title: Semantic similarity with sentence embeddings | Fast Data Science
excerpts:
Section Title: Semantic similarity with sentence embeddings
Content:
April 15, 2024 · Thomas Wood
In [natural language processing](https://fastdatascience.com/natural-language-processing/) , we have the concept of word vector embeddings and sentence embeddings. This is a vector, typically hundreds of numbers, which represents the meaning of a word or sentence.
Embeddings are useful because you can calculate [how similar two sentences are](https://fastdatascience.com/natural-language-processing/finding-similar-documents-nlp/) by converting them both to vectors, and calculating a distance metric.
Section Title: Semantic similarity with sentence embeddings
Content:
You can see below how two sentences can be converted to a vector, and we can measure the distance between them. Try playing with a few sentences in the Fast Data Science Sentence Embeddings Visualiser below - it’s really interesting to get some intuition for how vector embeddings behave. It may take about 30 seconds to load the transformer model. The model runs in your browser, which means it’s not the biggest or most powerful model out there!
Section Title: Semantic similarity with sentence embeddings > Fast Data Science Sentence Embeddings Visualiser
Content:
Enter two passages of text. What happens when you enter (a) identical texts, (b) very similar texts, e.g. "cats" and "kittens", (c) completely unrelated texts, or (d) opposites ( *black bag* vs *white bag* )? I like dogs and I like cats. I love all animals.
Please wait, loading model...
Section Title: Semantic similarity with sentence embeddings > 2D vector projection
Content:
Because we only have two vectors, we can display them on a 2D screen with the same angle between them as in the original 512-dimensional space!
[Click 'Calculate vectors' to see the similarity and visualization]
Section Title: Semantic similarity with sentence embeddings > ... > Vector 1 (512D full vector):

Section Title: Semantic similarity with sentence embeddings > Which sentence embedding model does this code use?
Content:
The above example uses the [Universal Sentence Encoder lite](https://github.com/tensorflow/tfjs-models/blob/master/universal-sentence-encoder/README.md) in Tensorflow JS, which runs in your browser and doesn’t send anything to a server. It uses vectors of size 512 (512-dimensional embeddings).
Section Title: Semantic similarity with sentence embeddings > Why would we want to use sentence embeddings?
Content:
Before embeddings, if you wanted to compare documents or texts to work out how similar they were, the easiest way was to count the words in common. This clearly falls over when documents don’t share any words in common, but use synonyms. You can read about ways of comparing text in our blog post on [finding similar documents in NLP](https://fastdatascience.com/natural-language-processing/finding-similar-documents-nlp/) .
Sentence embeddings mean that your entire document set can be converted to a set of vectors and stored like that, and any new document can be quickly compared to the ones in the index.
 ... 
Section Title: ... > How is the distance or similarity calculated between two sentence embedding vectors?
Content:
Two of the commonest ways of calculating the similarity between two sentence or word embedding vectors are the [Euclidean distance](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Euclidean_distance) and the [cosine similarity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cosine_similarity) . These are easier to understand in two dimensions.
Let’s imagine you want to compare two dissimilar sentences, and two similar sentences. If we imagine our vectors are only in two dimensions instead of 512 dimensions, our sentences might look like the below graphs.
The **Euclidean distance** (see the two graphs below) is just the straight-line distance between the two vectors. It is large when the two sentences are very different and small when they are similar.
*The Euclidean distance for two vectors which are close together is small.*
*The Euclidean distance for two vectors which are pointing in more different directions.*
Section Title: ... > How is the distance or similarity calculated between two sentence embedding vectors?
Content:
The **cosine similarity** (bottom two graphs) is a value between -1 and 1 and it’s the [dot product](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dot_product) ( *scalar product* ) of the two vectors, divided by the dot product of their lengths. For vectors of length 1, it’s the same as the dot product of the two vectors (you don’t need to divide by anything).
Similar sentences have a cosine similarity close to 1, whereas very different sentences have a similarity close to 0 or even negative. It’s quite rare to see cosine similarities that are close to -1.
*The cosine similarity is large or close to 1 for two vectors which are pointing in a similar direction, indicating semantic similarity.*
*The cosine similarity for two vectors which are pointing in very different directions is small. For vectors pointing in opposite directions, it would be negative.*
Section Title: ... > How is the distance or similarity calculated between two sentence embedding vectors?
Content:
Most sentence embedding models such as the [HuggingFace](https://huggingface.co/barissayil/bert-sentiment-analysis-sst) [transformer](https://deepai.org/machine-learning-glossary-and-terms/transformer-neural-network) models give all vectors of length 1, which means that you don’t need to calculate the bottom half of the fraction in the formula for the cosine similarity.
In the demonstration at the top of this page, we are calculating the cosine similarity.
Semantic similarity with NLP
Section Title: Semantic similarity with sentence embeddings > Need to compare documents on a semantic level?
Content:
We have developed systems for past clients that run on semantic similarity metrics with word2vec, doc2vec, or generative AI models, and can advise on or develop a semantic NLP solution for your business needs.
[Discuss semantic similarity and NLP with Fast Data Science](https://fastdatascience.com/contact)
 ...

Content:
